graphic.name: set-square
graphic.caption: A set-square is a drafting tool shaped like a 45° right triangle. Two special triangles like this show up so often that their side ratios are worth memorizing.

steps.body:
Two right triangles appear everywhere, so it pays to know them by heart.
The 45-45-90 is half of a square — two equal [legs](blue) and a [hypotenuse](green).
The 30-60-90 is half of an equilateral triangle — a [short leg](blue), a [long leg](orange), and the [hypotenuse](green).
A handy rule: the [short leg](blue) is always opposite the smallest angle.

steps.subtitle: Each clue describes one of the two special triangles. Tap a card, then tap the triangle it belongs to.

items.label: A short leg and a long leg

steps.doneNote: Sorted! The 45-45-90 is half a square (two equal legs, 1 : 1 : √2). The 30-60-90 is half an equilateral triangle (a short and a long leg, 1 : √3 : 2).

steps.subtitle: Make the two legs equal and the angles read 45°, 45°, 90°. Triangles with the same angles always share the same side ratios.

steps.body:
Two ratios to remember (√2 ≈ 1.41 and √3 ≈ 1.73):
45-45-90 → sides 1 : 1 : √2, so the [hypotenuse](green) is a [leg](blue) × √2.
30-60-90 → sides 1 : √3 : 2, so the [hypotenuse](green) is 2 × the [short leg](blue), and the [long leg](orange) is the [short leg](blue) × √3.

steps.prompt: Match each relationship to its value
steps.subtitle: Pin down the two special triangles. Tap a relationship, then its value.
steps.doneNote: Memorize these two ratios and the rest follows.

steps.prompt: Proof: from a square and an equilateral triangle

blanks.prompt: Cut a unit square along its diagonal d. By the Pythagorean theorem d² = 1² + 1² = ?
An equilateral triangle has three equal angles that add to 180°. Each one is 180 ÷ 3 = ?
Drop the height h of that side-2 triangle. By the Pythagorean theorem h² = 2² − 1² = ?

stages: Start with a square of side 1 and draw a diagonal. It splits the square into two identical right triangles, each with two 45° angles.
By the Pythagorean theorem the diagonal is √(1² + 1²) = √2. So a 45-45-90 triangle has sides 1 : 1 : √2.
Now take an equilateral triangle of side 2 — every angle 60° — and drop a height from the top. It cuts the base into two 1's and splits the top angle into two 30°s.
By the Pythagorean theorem that height is √(2² − 1²) = √3. So a 30-60-90 triangle has sides 1 : √3 : 2.

steps.subtitle: A student works with a 30-60-90 triangle whose short leg is 5. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: 30-60-90 triangle, short leg = 5 (opposite the 30 degree angle).

steps.explanation: The long leg is the short leg times sqrt(3) (about 1.73), not times 2. So it is 5 x sqrt(3), which is about 8.66. Only the hypotenuse is 2x the short leg — they can't both be 10.
steps.hint: Which side is twice the short leg — the hypotenuse or the long leg? Only one of them can be.
steps.doneNote: Right — the long leg is the short leg × √3 (≈ 8.66); only the hypotenuse is 2× the short leg.

problems.prompt: A 45-45-90 right triangle has legs of length 5. Find the hypotenuse (in terms of √2).

feedback.correct: Correct. The hypotenuse is a leg times √2: 5√2.
feedback.incorrect: In a 45-45-90 triangle the hypotenuse equals a leg times √2.

problems.prompt: A 30-60-90 right triangle has a short leg of 6 (opposite the 30° angle). Find the hypotenuse.

feedback.correct: Correct. The hypotenuse is twice the short leg: 2 × 6 = 12.
feedback.incorrect: In a 30-60-90 triangle the hypotenuse is twice the short leg.

problems.prompt: A 30-60-90 right triangle has a short leg of 4. Find the long leg (in terms of √3).

feedback.correct: Correct. The long leg is the short leg times √3: 4√3.
feedback.incorrect: In a 30-60-90 triangle the long leg (opposite 60°) is the short leg times √3.

problems.strongHint: Long leg = short leg × √3 = 4√3, so enter 4.

steps.subtitle: A 30-60-90 triangle has a hypotenuse of 14. Find the short leg (opposite the 30° angle).

feedback.correct: Done. The short leg is half the hypotenuse: 14 ÷ 2 = 7.
feedback.incorrect: In a 30-60-90 triangle the short leg is half the hypotenuse.
